sections.title: Jeff Kaplan: Game Design Philosophy -- Canonical Reference
sections.summary: Compiled from GDC talks, DICE keynotes, interviews, developer updates, forum posts, the Lex Fridman Podcast #493 (March 2026), and various press interviews across 2005-2026. ---

sections.title: 4. Blizzard Core Design Mantras
sections.summary: These were company-wide philosophies that Kaplan worked within. Articulated primarily by Rob Pardo in GDC/AGC talks, but Kaplan embodied them in practice:

sections.title: "Concentrated Coolness"
sections.summary: > "Rather than making as much variety and many different things as you can, make sure > the things you do make are as cool as possible." Applied to WoW: 9 distinct classes rather t

sections.title: "Easy to Learn, Difficult to Master"
sections.summary: > "The first thing we always do is design the depth first and the accessibility later." The approach ensures engaging core mechanics exist before simplifying the interface. Appeals

sections.title: "Killing with a Purpose"
sections.summary: Quest design philosophy providing narrative reasons for combat beyond XP. Instead of grinding: "You're always moving around the world, seeing a new place."

sections.title: "Purity in Purpose"
sections.summary: Maintaining design integrity by avoiding compromises. Blizzard refused creating solo dungeons despite requests, preserving group content's prestige rather than diluting both experi

sections.title: Solo-ability as Baseline
sections.summary: A core goal for WoW: the game would be solo-able to level 60, making it available to everyone so players could experience it like a single-player game if they wanted. ---

sections.summary: > "People are just horrible to each other... It's because we've dehumanised the other > person and there's anonymity to it."

sections.title: The Developer-Community Paradox
sections.summary: > "But the second you go, 'Let's try to be a little bit cooler about what we do with > the community,' we actually get destroyed by that very community."

sections.title: On Developers and Value
